rules.description: Fuseki candidates prefer efficient corner claims, side extensions, stable shape, and influence only when the whole-board balance supports it.
Middlegame move scores emphasize group safety, liberties, cutting points, connection value, sente, and large-scale territory versus influence tradeoffs.
Yose play uses score lead, ownership estimate, local sente/gote value, boundary reduction, ko risk, and pass timing from territory calculation.
When remaining legal plays only fill secure own territory, do not self-fill; pass and enter graph-area scoring unless an urgent capture, atari defense, ko, or neutral yose remains.
Use joseki and fuseki books as priors rather than fixed scripts. Search should compare candidate moves by ownership estimate, group safety, and whole-board value before committing.
Training examples should preserve ownership, score lead, and liberty features because they stabilize move selection better than raw win/loss labels alone.
